title: Federalism: Essentials & Core Relations
lead: ==Federalism is basic structure== — [[Kesavananda Bharati (1973)]], [[SR Bommai (1994)]]. ==K. C. Wheare== famously called India ==‘quasi-federal’==; C. H. Alexandrowicz asked whether India should be called a federation at all. The ==end of one-party rule in 1967== was the turning point in federal aspirations.
intro: SC in 2024: States can collect royalty AND tax major minerals, and can regulate industrial alcohol — opening a significant revenue source for states.

items.title: Art 245 — Territory
bullets: Parliament: laws for ==whole/part of the territory of India==
States: laws for their own territory

items.title: Art 246 — Three Lists
bullets: ==Union List== → Parliament; ==State List== → States; ==Concurrent== → both (Parl > States)

items.title: Art 248 — Residuary
bullets: Subjects in no list → ==only Parliament== (supported by ==Entry 97, List I==)

items.title: Art 254 — Repugnancy
bullets: Inconsistency between Union & State laws on the Concurrent List → ==Union law prevails==

items.label: Union List subjects — national importance, uniform laws
State List subjects — local importance, exclusive state power
Concurrent List subjects — both legislate, Parliament prevails

head: Case
Holding
rows: The connection must be ==real, not illusory==, and the liability imposed must be ==pertinent to that connection==.
Nexus theory doesn't impose a tax — it indicates the circumstances in which a tax ==may be enforced==.
State can legislate on trusts ==within its territory== even if some trust property lies in another State.
Extra-territorial laws must have a ==nexus with something in India==.
